title: Truncation Bias
concept: Setting the maximum sequence length too short cuts off critical context at the end of documents, leading to incorrect predictions.

exercises.prompt: What happens when you read only the first half of a long story?

options.text: Missing the ending can lead to wrong conclusions.
The end of a text is never important.
Computers can read minds and guess the missing parts.

exercises.explain: Just like in human reading, if we don't look at the entire sentence or document, we might miss the main point and make a wrong assumption.

cards.title: Don't Cut Me Off!
cards.body: Have you ever had someone cut you off mid-sentence? It's frustrating because they miss your main point! Machine learning models experience the exact same thing when we set their text limits too short.

cards.title: What is Truncation Bias?
cards.body: Truncation bias happens when we set our model's maximum sequence length too short. This cuts off (truncates) the end of our text data, meaning the AI never gets to see critical context or final conclusions, leading to bad predictions.

cards.title: The Ripped Book
cards.body: Imagine trying to read a murder mystery novel, but the publisher ripped out the last chapter. You know all the clues, but you miss the big reveal at the end! That is truncation bias—your AI model is forced to guess the ending because it was cut off.

cards.title: The Cut-Off Review
cards.body: If you feed a customer review into a model with a short limit: "I hated the setup, it took hours and I wanted to return it. But after using it, it is actually the best purchase I have ever made!" \n\nIf truncated, the AI only reads "I hated the setup..." and incorrectly flags it as a negative review.

cards.title: Myth: The End Doesn't Matter
cards.body: Many think that cutting off long text only loses "extra fluff" and saves computer memory. In reality, the most important information—like the final decision in a contract or the resolution of a story—is often at the very end!

cards.title: Visualizing the Cut
cards.body: See how setting a short text limit changes what the model actually reads and understands.

left.text: "The movie started slow and boring, but the ending was absolutely brilliant!" -> AI predicts POSITIVE.

right.text: "The movie started slow and boring..." [CUT OFF] -> AI predicts NEGATIVE.

exercises.prompt: What actually happens to a long document when you set a very short maximum sequence length?

options.text: It cuts off the text and discards everything past the limit.
It compresses the entire text to fit the size.
It translates the text into a shorter language.
It alerts the user to write shorter sentences.

exercises.explain: Setting the maximum sequence length too short forces the model to ignore any words that come after that limit, cutting off the end of your data.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Setting a longer maximum sequence length increases the risk of truncation bias.
exercises.explain: Truncation bias is caused by setting the sequence limit too short, which deletes the end of the text. Setting a longer limit allows the model to see the whole picture.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to describe this text-chopping problem.
exercises.sentence: Cutting off the end of a document due to short sequence limits causes ________ bias.
exercises.explain: Truncation bias happens when we chop off the end of our input text, hiding the final context from the AI.

exercises.prompt: Which system correctly explains the root cause of truncation bias?

options.text: System A says truncation bias happens because the model is physically missing the cut-off text context.
System B says truncation bias happens because the model's brain gets too full and it forgets the beginning.
System C says truncation bias only happens when the text has spelling mistakes.

exercises.explain: System A correctly points out that truncation bias occurs because the AI lacks the full context of the text. System B incorrectly blames the AI's internal memory capacity.

exercises.prompt: Why does setting a small maximum sequence length cause a classifier to misgrade a student's long essay?

options.text: The model processes the entire document but gets confused by too many details.
The model gets slower because it has to read the entire long essay.
The model misses crucial details at the end of the text and makes an incorrect guess.
The model automatically summarizes the missing text using its own imagination.

exercises.explain: Truncation bias occurs because the sequence limit cuts off the end of the text. If a student's answer or crucial conclusion is at the very end of a long essay, a truncated model will completely miss it and make an incorrect prediction based on incomplete info.
